facts_disclosed: Switched to Romo Fabrics in UK — better commercial range and pricing

turns.content: I have made a significant change to our supply chain and I want to think through the implications properly. We have moved our primary fabric sourcing to a new supplier.
That's a major operational decision. What prompted the change, and who are you moving to?

We have shifted to Romo Fabrics in the United Kingdom. For years I sourced primarily through a Parisian house — Maison Pierre — and their quality is undeniable. Their silk-wool blends, their bespoke dye work, everything about them speaks to craftsmanship. But craftsmanship at boutique scale does not always serve a firm that is growing.

That's a tension many growing design firms face — the supplier that was perfect when you were doing a handful of bespoke residential projects may not have the capacity or pricing structure to support a larger volume of work. What specifically about Romo made them the right choice?

Several things. First, their contract-grade range is substantially deeper than what Maison Pierre offers. We have been taking on projects that require fabrics to meet commercial durability standards — higher Martindale abrasion ratings, fire retardancy certifications, that sort of rigour. Maison Pierre's strength is in luxury residential textiles, not in performance-rated materials. Romo has an entire division dedicated to contract-grade fabrics without sacrificing design quality.

That makes a lot of sense. The contract-grade requirements are a fundamentally different category from luxury residential textiles — you need fabrics that can perform in high-traffic, high-use environments while still meeting your aesthetic standards. Romo does have a strong reputation in that space, particularly their Black Edition and Kirkby Design lines. Was the pricing structure also a factor?

Very much so. As our project scale has increased, the volume of fabric we order has grown considerably. Maison Pierre prices for exclusivity — their margins are built for small, bespoke orders. Romo offers a pricing structure that is far more sustainable at the quantities we now require. I am not interested in cheap fabric — I would never compromise on that — but I am interested in pricing that reflects a genuine commercial partnership rather than a boutique transaction.

The distinction between boutique transaction pricing and commercial partnership pricing is an important one. At volume, even a modest per-metre difference compounds significantly across a large project. And Romo's scale means they can offer volume pricing without compromising quality because their production infrastructure supports it.

How has the transition been practically? Changing your primary fabric supplier affects sample libraries, lead times, client presentations — a lot of downstream processes.

The transition has been methodical. I spent two months evaluating their range before committing. I ordered sample books across their full portfolio — the Mark Alexander line, the Villa Nova collection, the contract ranges. I tested them against our current projects to see whether the colour depth and hand quality met our standards. They did. In some cases they exceeded what I had been working with. The lead times from the UK are also more reliable than what I was experiencing from Paris, which is a practical consideration I cannot ignore.

A two-month evaluation period before committing shows real discipline — you weren't reacting to a single frustration but making a considered strategic decision. The fact that lead times from the UK are actually more reliable than from Paris is interesting and probably reflects Romo's operational scale versus a smaller Parisian atelier.

Mark Alexander and Villa Nova are both strong lines within the Romo portfolio — Mark Alexander especially has that textural depth that would align with high-end residential and hospitality work. Are you maintaining any relationship with Maison Pierre for specific applications, or is this a clean break?

It is not a complete severance. There are applications where Maison Pierre remains unmatched — their hand-dyed silks, for example, have a luminosity that no production-scale manufacturer can replicate. For a statement drapery in a private residence, I would still turn to them. But as our primary, day-to-day supplier for the majority of our textile needs, Romo is now the relationship I am building. The volume will flow through them.

That's a smart hybrid approach — Romo as the workhorse for the majority of your textile sourcing, with Maison Pierre reserved for those exceptional, bespoke moments where nothing else will do. It protects your quality ceiling while giving you a commercially viable foundation.

Have you assigned a dedicated account manager at Romo? For a relationship you're planning to build at volume, having a single point of contact who understands your standards — the way Julien presumably did at Maison Pierre — makes an enormous difference.

I have. Their regional representative for the Gulf, a woman named Catherine, has been excellent. She spent a full day at our studio understanding our project types, our palette tendencies, our quality thresholds. That kind of investment in the relationship impressed me. She did not simply send a catalogue and a price list. She wanted to understand how we work. That is how you earn a designer's loyalty.

That's exactly the kind of supplier relationship that sustains itself — someone who invests in understanding your design language rather than just fulfilling orders. Catherine spending a full day at your studio to understand your approach mirrors the kind of client-first thinking you probably bring to your own work. It suggests Romo takes the partnership seriously at a strategic level, not just a transactional one.

It sounds like this transition has been well-considered and well-executed. The commercial logic is clear, the quality evaluation was rigorous, and the relationship is already being built with intention.

It has been one of the more consequential operational decisions I have made this year. A supplier relationship in design is not just logistics — it shapes what is possible. The fabrics available to me define the palette, the texture, the weight of every room I design. Changing that foundation is not something I undertook lightly. But the firm is evolving, and the supply chain must evolve with it.

That's a profound point — the supplier doesn't just deliver materials, they define the boundaries of your design vocabulary. And when the firm's trajectory changes, the supply chain has to keep pace or it becomes a constraint rather than an enabler. Have you thought about how this might affect your design process day to day? Working with a new sample library, new swatch books — there's always an adjustment period.

There is, and I welcome it. A new sample library is like a new colour palette — it opens possibilities I would not have considered before. I spent an evening last week simply laying out Romo swatches on the studio table, pairing textures, testing combinations under different light. It was the most creatively energising thing I have done in months. When you work with the same supplier for years, you begin to design within their vocabulary unconsciously. A new vocabulary forces you to think freshly. That is a gift, not a burden.

That's a wonderful way to frame it — and the fact that you found the process creatively energising rather than disruptive says a lot about the quality of what Romo offers. Designing within a supplier's unconscious vocabulary is a real phenomenon, and breaking out of it can unlock directions you wouldn't have found otherwise. It sounds like Romo gives you room to grow while maintaining the quality standard your work demands.

That is exactly it. Room to grow without compromising. Thank you for helping me articulate the reasoning clearly. When I present this change to my team in full context, I want the logic to be airtight.

The logic is solid: commercial-grade range, sustainable volume pricing, reliable lead times, creative renewal, and a supplier who invested in understanding your practice. That's a compelling case for your team. The fact that Maison Pierre remains available for exceptional applications means you haven't lost anything — you've gained capacity. Good luck with the team presentation.
